coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional core-shell silica microspheres and their performance in self-carrier decomposition, sustained drug release and fluorescent bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Mehdi, Yamina Ait
Itatahine, Asma
Fizir, Meriem
Xiao, Deli
Dramou, Pierre
He, Hua
coredata.dc:description: An ideal nanocarrier system for drug delivery is that one made from biocompatible and biodegradable materials for safe excretion from the biological system, and often with additional imaging abilities. In the present work, new core-shell silica microspheres have been prepared, with carrier decomposition after drug release. Paclitaxel, which is one of the most efficient drugs against a wide range of malignancies was integrated into the silica core. The carrier decomposition resulted from the escape of drug molecules with loading capacity about 16.95%. To achieve the fluorescents properties of the synthesized material a biocompatible photoluminescent prepared carbon dots were inserted in a silica shell around the Ptx-SiO2 core. The resultant silica core-shell (Ptx-SiO2CDs-SiO2) NPs with average particle size around ~100 nm showed high fluorescent properties from the confocal laser scanning microscope observation. Further observation under UV-light at 365 nm also confirmed the photoluminescence. The Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs were highly water soluble, and provide a sustained drug release as well as pH sensitivity. The incubation of A549 cells line with Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs exhibits high cellular uptake as shown by CDs imaging. These properties in addition to the biocompatibility of Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs and biodegradability of the silica core contributed simultaneously with the drug release process for easy body excretion after its functionality via renal system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Core-Shell silica microspheres
Carrier self-decomposition
Sustained release
Cellular uptake
Bio-imaging
Biocompatibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of encapsulation efficiency and degree of drug loading
Incorporation of CDs NPs into the silica shell around the Ptx-SiO2 microspheres
Incorporation of CDs NPs into the silica shell around the Ptx-SiO2 microspheres

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Reaction scheme of functionalization of Ptx-SiO2.
(A) FTIR spectra for CDs and APTES; (B) Emission spectra of CDs in ethanol solution (when excited between 340 and 500 nm).
TEM images of the prepared Ptx-SiO2 NPs with scale bare (a) 20 nm and (b) 50 nm.
SEM images of the prepared Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 with scale bare of (a) 1 µm and (b) 500 nm. TEM images of Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 with scale bare of (c) 20 nm and (d) 50 nm.

Photos of the prepared CDs dispersed in ethanol, Ptx-SiO2, Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 solutions in the bright field (a), and under UV light at 365 nm (b), (c) Fluorescence optical image with a scale bar of 20 µm.

(A) Release of Ptx from the Ptx-SiO2 and Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs at pH 7.4, (B) the release of Ptx from Ptx-SiO2 NPs in PBS with different pH values (pH 7.4 and pH 5.0), (C) the release of Ptx from Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 in PBS pH 7.4 and serum (50% PBS pH 7.4).
TEM images of Ptx-SiO@CDs-SiO2 NPs after their immersion in buffer solution PBS (pH 7.4) at 37 °C for (a) 1, (b) 4, (c) 8, (d) 15 days respectively, with a scale bar of 100 nm.
CLSM images of A549 cells pretreated with (A) Ptx-SiO2, (B) Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 for 2 h at 37 °C under different excitation wavelengths of 400, 440 and 500 nm.
Cell viability of SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs towards A549 cells incubated with NPs at different concentrations for 24 and 48 h.
Variation of drug encapsulation efficiency (EE) and degree of drug loading (DDL %) in function of drug amount on different samples.
In vitro drug release data for every model.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional core-shell silica microspheres and their performance in self-carrier decomposition, sustained drug release and fluorescent bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, School of Sciences, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, School of Sciences, China Pharmaceutical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An ideal nanocarrier system for drug delivery is that one made from biocompatible and biodegradable materials for safe excretion from the biological system, and often with additional imaging abilities. In the present work, new core-shell silica microspheres have been prepared, with carrier decomposition after drug release. Paclitaxel, which is one of the most efficient drugs against a wide range of malignancies was integrated into the silica core. The carrier decomposition resulted from the escape of drug molecules with loading capacity about 16.95%. To achieve the fluorescents properties of the synthesized material a biocompatible photoluminescent prepared carbon dots were inserted in a silica shell around the Ptx-SiO2 core. The resultant silica core-shell (Ptx-SiO2CDs-SiO2) NPs with average particle size around ~100 nm showed high fluorescent properties from the confocal laser scanning microscope observation. Further observation under UV-light at 365 nm also confirmed the photoluminescence. The Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs were highly water soluble, and provide a sustained drug release as well as pH sensitivity. The incubation of A549 cells line with Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs exhibits high cellular uptake as shown by CDs imaging. These properties in addition to the biocompatibility of Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs and biodegradability of the silica core contributed simultaneously with the drug release process for easy body excretion after its functionality via renal system.

A novel core-shell silica microspheres with small particle size, excellent biocompatibility, high fluorescence properties and carrier self-decomposition ability was developed.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Paclitaxel (Ptx) is considered as one of the most effective chemotherapeutic drugs ever developed and is active against a wide range of cancers, such as lung, ovarian, and breast cancers [1,2]. Mechanistically, Ptx arrests the cell cycle and induces cell death by stabilizing microtubules and interfering with microtubule disassembly in cell division [3]. Currently, Ptx is available in a formulation, which contains a mixture of Cremophor EL as a solubilizer and ethanol (50:50, v/v) a combination known as Taxol [4]. However, clinical application of this formulation is often accompanied by severe, undesirable side-effects, hair loss, muscle and joint pains, and diarrhea, among others. It results in a greater risk of infections that can potentially be serious, cardiac toxicity, allergic reaction, gastrointestinal reaction, neurotoxicity, in addition to the low solubility of this drug in water which is a major limitation in its clinical use [5].

Many systems and nanocarriers have been developed to protect the drug from degradation during circulation and in-turn protect the body from toxic side effects of the drug, thereby lowering its toxicity, and improve its water solubility to facilitate the administration of this formulation. Biodegradable polymeric devices have been used to encapsulate Ptx, such as Polylactide-Poly-Ethylene-Glycol (PLA-PEG) micelles, poly (lactic-co-glycolic acid ) (PLGA) nanoparticles embedded with paclitaxel and coated with hyaluronic acid (HA-PTX-PLGA) to actively target the drug to a triple negative breast cancer cells [6,7]. Polyelectrolyte nanocapsules formed from bio-acceptable and approved chemicals have been used to improve the drug solubility [8]. Ptx loaded Transferrin (Tf)-modified poly(D, L-lactic-co-glycolic acid) nanoparticles (PLGA NPs) and superparamagnetic nanoparticle (MNP) [9], and also carbon nanocarriers, where Ptx have been loaded on carbon nanotubes. A novel single-walled carbon nanotube (SWNT)-based drug delivery system was developed by conjugation of human serum albumin (HSA) nanoparticles for loading of Ptx anticancer agent [10].

The main challenge of an ideal drug carrier is processing several features in the same time, counting effective cellular uptake, controllable release, facile excretion from the biological system after therapeutic effect, and enhanced drug efficacy. The last two aspects are considered as most important drawbacks in nanomedicine development. To achieve easier cellular uptake, many strategies have been developed to attend hydrophilic surface of nanocarriers. And also to achieve the aspect of the controllable release of drug molecules from the carrier to guarantee relatively stable therapeutic levels during the treatment period [11–13].

Silica microspheres have been considered as one of the most promising carrier systems, due to its biocompatibility, and it is providing a versatile platform for drug loading [14]. Three generic strategies have been developed for drug loading into silica carrier, with the drug molecules being embedded in the dense SiO2 NP, residing in the pores of the mesoporous SiO2 NP, and being attached to the surface of NPs, via chemical bonding or physical adsorption. The hollow silica microspheres have also been used as promising nanocarriers [15–17].

The hydrophilic surface of SiO2 microspheres make these nanocarriers favorable for cellular uptake [18], it consists of two types of functional groups, siloxane (Si-O-Si) and silanol (Si-OH) [19,20]. Which provide the functionalization by using various kind of functional nanoparticles such as, magnetic and fluorescent nanoparticles on the surface or inside the silica materials, to achieve the mentioned properties accompanied by the drug delivery. Magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4 or Fe2O3 ) have been used to functionalize silica microspheres for external magnetic field targeting [21–23] and also to reach the fluorescent properties the silica materials have been modified by organic dyes [24,25], semiconducting quantum dots (QDs) [26], and noble metal nanoparticles [27–29] used for cell bio-imaging. However, these materials have a certain limitation for biomedicine applications. Organic dye show poor photostability and potential toxicity, QDs have problems associated with the inherent heavy metal toxicity, and noble metal materials involve high cost. In addition to the preparation of these NPs involves complicated multi-step synthetic procedures.

The recently explored carbon-based quantum dots consisting of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CDs), with tunable emission as a new type of carbon nanomaterials with a particle size below 10 nm, considered as fluorescence marker in nanobiotechnology. It is proved that CDs are beneficial over conventional organic dyes in terms of optical brightness, photostability, and also high water solubility, and over semiconductor QDs concerning material toxicity [30,31], eco-friendliness, and low cost. CDs are generally used as biosensors for analytical applications (determination of elements) [32–34], but recently many progress in the synthesis, modifications, and applications of CDs have been made with the aim of using CDs in the biomedical field for cell bio-imaging and as biosensors agent [35–39]. Many materials are associated with CDs to be used in the biomedical applications. Practically, CDs have been coupled with magnetic iron oxide (Fe3O4) NPs to attain both of magnetic and fluorescent properties in one hybrid nanoparticles [33]. CDs have been also grafted onto the surface of polymeric materials, such as mesoporous silica microspheres, for cell bio-imaging and redox-responsive controlled drug (Doxorubicin) delivery [40].

Recently, some works are focused on the feature of carrier excretion from the blood system after drug release. This aim has been achieved by two ways: drug release leads to carrier decomposition by drug molecules reside inside the carrier thus the drug molecules escape generate the carrier networks decomposition into small fragments easy to renal elimination [41–43]. The other way is by using biodegradable materials in intracellular (cancer cells ) conditions (acidic pH, enzymes) [44,45].

To address the safe carrier excretion issue, enhance drug efficacy and reduce Ptx collateral effects, we have developed a special type of self-decomposable SiO2-drug composite NPs, by creating a radial concentration gradient of the drug in the NP. Drug release occurred simultaneously with the silica carrier decomposition. Such unique characteristic was different from the conventional dense SiO2-drug NP, in which drug was uniformly distributed and can hardly escape the carrier. In the present work, a facile and efficient method of preparation of fluorescent silica core-shell was reported, by loading Ptx in the silica core with the drug particles being embedded inside the dense silica NPs using a reported method [16], then incorporating of the highly photo-luminescent synthesized CDs with silane groups via one-pot reaction on the silica shell around the Ptx-SiO2 core. The conditions of silica shell growth process in the reported method (fabrication of multifunctional silica core-shell incorporated with CdSe/CdZns QDs and γ-Fe2O3) [46] have been optimized in an aim to preserve the photo-luminescent properties, with negligible drug loss assured by the coating of the drug inside the silica NPs. The drug loading efficiency and the drug release were evaluated and studied for both synthesized materials. Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 and Ptx-SiO2 NPs were incubated with A549 (adenocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial) cells line for preliminary studies about cellular uptake, which were investigated using CDs imaging. The biocompatibility studies of the unloaded SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs were carried out by, incubation of these NPs with A549 cells at different concentrations during 24 and 48 h.

Paclitaxel, Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), ammonia-water solution (NH4-OH 28%), citric acid anhydrous, 3-aminopropyl-triethoxysilane (APTES), hydroxypropyl cellulose (HPC), petroleum ether, methanol, and ethanol were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Inc. (Shanghai, China), and were of analytical reagent grade used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water.

CDs nanoparticles were successfully synthesized using the solvothermal process [47], as explored in Fig. 1. In a typical process, 10 mL of APTES were placed in 100 mL three-necked flask and the system was kept under nitrogen gas flow for about 10 min in order to degas APTES then heated at 240 °C, followed by addition of 0.5 g of citric acid anhydrous to the flask with vigorous stirring. The system was kept at this temperature for 1 min till the yellowish color of CDs appeared. After cooling down to room temperature the synthesized CDs were dispersed in ethanol, and the excess of citric acid was precipitated by addition of a certain volume of petroleum ether. The resultant CDs yellowish precipitation was extracted by a certain amount of ethanol for the next experiments.

The Ptx-SiO2 NPs were synthesized according to the previously reported method [16], with using Ptx instead of Dox. In a typical procedure, a certain amount of Ptx (2, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 15, 20 and 25 mg) for samples D1, D2, D3, D4, D5, D6, D7, D8, was first added to a mixture of 75 mL ethanol with 3.4 mL of 25% ammonia–water solution subsequently, 0.08 mL TEOS was added. After 24 h of stirring the resultant Ptx-SiO2 NPs have been washed several times before being dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of encapsulation efficiency and degree of drug loading
ce:section.ce:para: The Ptx-SiO2 NPs (D1-D8), 10 mg of each sample were immersed in 10 mL of methanol separately for 24 h at 50 °C, and then ultrasonicated for 1 h to allow Ptx fully escape from the silica nanospheres for all the eight samples. Subsequently, the washed Ptx from the microspheres was separated from the mixture by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 20 min. The determination of drug loading efficiency by HPLC was triplicated. The HPLC was calibrated with standard solutions of 5–100 μg/mL of Ptx dissolved in methanol [48] with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9993.

The amount of drug inside Ptx-SiO2 NPs was determined by the drug Encapsulation Efficiency (EE %) (Eq. (1)) and by Degree of Drug Loading (DDL %) (Eq. (2))
(1)
EE
( % )
=
amount of drug in
sample
amount of used drug
× 100
(2)
DDL
( % )
=
C
×
V
M
× 100
Where C is the concentration of Ptx in the supernatant (mg/mL), V is the volume of the supernatant and M is the mass of the loaded Ptx-SiO2 NPs (mg).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Incorporation of CDs NPs into the silica shell around the Ptx-SiO2 microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for incorporating of CDs NPs into silica microspheres was adapted from the literature [46] by using CDs instead of CdSe/CdZnS core/shell quantum dots (QDs), and also by changing the bare SiO2 microspheres in this reported method by the previously prepared Ptx-SiO2 NPs. In a typical procedure, 30 mg of Ptx-SiO2 NPs and 16 mg of hydroxypropyl cellulose (HPC) were dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol, and this mixture was ultrasonicated for 10 min. Different volumes of CDs solution (50, 100, 150, 200 µL) were added to the reaction mixture while the solution was vigorously stirred, followed by the addition of 50 µL of H2O, 50 µL of NH4-OH, (28 wt% in H2O) and (100, 150, 200, 250 µL) of TEOS to study the influence of CDs and TEOS amounts on the fluorescent properties. The mixture was stirred in an oil bath at 75 °C for 4 h. The core-shell silica microspheres (Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2) were then purified by performing five cycles of centrifuging, discarding the supernatant, and redispersing the microspheres in water. The reaction scheme for this process is shown in Fig. 2.

The morphology of the synthesized NPs was observed by using an S-3000 scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi Corporation, Japan) and an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM). The samples for SEM observation were prepared as follows: first the sample was dispersed in ethanol and a drop of the dispersion was put on a copper stub and dried; subsequently, the sample was coated with a thin layer of gold film. Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometric (FT-IR) spectra were measured on an 8400 s FT-IR spectrometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). Fluorescence spectra of the prepared CDs were measured on a Shimadzu RF-5301 (PC) S (Japan) spectrofluorometer at room temperature. Fluorescent images were investigated using a Carl Zeiss Jena LSM700 (Germany) confocal laser scanning microscope. The samples were prepared by dispersing the microspheres in ethanol then spin coated onto glass microscope slides, and the photographs were taken with a Canon digital camera of suspensions of different materials in bright field and under UV light using a ZF-90 UV transilluminator (SH1717, China).

The HPLC analysis was performed on a Shimadzu LC-20AT HPLC system, with a binary pump and a diode array detector (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). A Shimadzu VP-ODS C18 (5 µm particle size, 250 mm × 4.6 mm) reverse phase column. The mobile phase consisted of methanol/water (70:30 v/v). The column temperature was maintained at 30 °C. The flow rate was set at 1.0 mL/min and the detection wavelength was 227 nm. Samples were injected at a volume of 50 µL [48].

In vitro drug release tests were carried out using the thermostatic Shaker at 37 °C and stirred at 100 rpm, by dissolving certain amount of Ptx-SiO2 and Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 in 15 mL of phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (pH 7.4 normal physiological conditions, pH 5.0 intracellular conditions of cancer cells), in order to compare both nanospheres release amounts and pH-sensitive drug release. In another hand the pH was controlled at 7.4, and PBS, serum (fetal bovine serum, 50% in PBS pH 7.4) mediums were used for the inspection of drug release from Ptx-SiO2 and Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs, by using dialysis bags (MWCO 3700 Da) containing suspensions of Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs in 2 mL of the above-mentioned mediums were placed in 20 mL of pH 7.4 PBS solution also using thermostatic Shaker at 37

℃ and stirred at 100 rpm [16]. A series of aliquots (4 mL) of the solution was removed at different time points. The HPLC analysis was performed for each aliquot after centrifugation at 1200 rpm for 20 min.

The kinetics theoretical models were applied for further study the release behavior and the release mechanism of Ptx from the nanocarriers. The calculation methods are investigated in the following equations:

Zero-order model
(3)
f
t
=
K
0
t
Where f t is the fraction of drug dissolved in time t, K0 is the zero-order release constant.

First order model
(4)
f
t
= 1 −
e
−
K
1
t
K1 is the first order release constant.

Higuchi model
(5)
f
t
=
K
H
t
0.5
Where KH is the Higuchi release constant.

Korsmeyer–Peppas model
(6)
f
t
=
K
p
t
n
Where Kp is the constant incorporating the structural and geometric characteristics of the drug dosage form; n is the release exponent, characteristic of the release mechanism. This equation is only applicable to the first 60% of the release profiles.
In vitro degradation behaviors of Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs were studied by immersing corresponding powders in buffer solution ( PBS pH 7.4) at 37 °C. The change of morphology was observed by TEM.

The A549 cells were cultured in DMEM culture medium constituted with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin/streptomycin at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2. The cellular uptake of both Ptx-SiO2 and Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs samples by A549 cells will be investigated using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). The cells were seeded in 24-well plates with a sterile round glass dish at a density of 1 × 105 cells/well were incubated for 24 h. Then Ptx-SiO2 and Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs at a final concentration of 25 μg/mL were added to the corresponding cell wells. After incubating for 2 h at 37 °C, the supernatant was removed and the cells were washed with cold PBS three times and fixed with 4% formaldehyde. Afterward, DAPI was used to stain the nuclei and the fixed cells were visualized by Leica DM-6000 CS microscope (Leica Instruments Inc., Wetzlar, Germany), at different excitations wavelengths (400 nm, 440 nm, 500 nm).

The cytotoxic effects of blank SiO2@CDs-SiO2 (non-loaded) samples on A549 cells will be evaluated by MTT assay. The cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well. After incubation for 24 h, the culture medium was removed and fresh DMEM containing serial concentrations of SiO2@SiO2-CDs (0.1, 1.0, 5.0, 10, 15, 20, 25, 50, 100 μg/mL) in PBS formulations were added. After an incubation of 24 h, 48 h, 50 µL of the MTT solution (2 mg/mL MTT in PBS, pH 7.4) was added to each well and the plate was incubated for further 4 h. After incubation, the culture medium containing MTT was removed by rinsing with fresh PBS. Then the intracellular formazan crystals were extracted by adding 150 µL of DMSO into the plate. The absorbance was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader (SpectraMax M3, Molecular Devices, USA).

The synthesis process of highly photo-luminescent carbon dots was adapted from a reported work with few modifications [47], which is illustrated in Fig. 1. Methyldimethoxy amide groups functionalized CDs were synthesized via a one-pot reaction, with basic synthesize of decomposition and pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid, simultaneously with the surface passivation reaction of the amine group of APTES with the carboxylic group gotten by the pyrolysis products. The acylation reaction of the surface is confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy for the synthesized CDs compared with pure APTES. Apparent absorption peaks at 1650 cm−1 and 1565 cm−1 of amide I and amide II, respectively were observed in Fig. 3. (A), the two signals arising from C=ONR stretching mode confirm the successful surface passivation reaction of CDs.

For the exploration of the optical properties of the synthesized CDs, a detailed fluorescence study was carried out under different excitation wavelengths. As illustrated in Fig. 3. (B) the synthesized CDs show a strong blue photoluminescence for excitation wavelength range from 340 nm to 400 nm and the emission is highly depended on the excitation energy, but when the excitation wavelength is over than 440 nm, the emission pick was shifted to the longer wavelength (over than 500 nm) then the fluorescence intensity decreased. Which indicates a distribution of the different surface energy traps of the CDs [30,47].

The 80 nm sized Ptx-SiO2 as shown in Fig. 4 were obtained by hydrolyzing of TEOS in the presence of ammonia-water solution, where the Ptx particles being embedded inside the silica microsphere. The Ptx-SiO2 have the spherical form and smooth surface with small size distribution. At the beginning of the reaction of silica core formation, Ptx molecules were linked to the silica species produced by TEOS hydrolysis via hydrogen bonds due to the presence of hydroxyl groups (-OH) on the surface of Ptx, then followed by further growth of Ptx-SiO2 NPs [16]. This method of Ptx loading decrease the drug loss and increase the incorporation efficiency [48].
The EE(%) and drug loading(%) are described in Table 1.

As shown in Table 1, when the amount of the used drug (Ptx) during the encapsulation was less than 20 mg, EE is almost 100% which means that all the used drug was encapsulated inside the silica nanoparticles, and for the Ptx amount over than 20 mg the encapsulation efficiency was 68.48%. The degree of drug loading also increases to attend 16.95% which is the maximum of the degree of drug loading. The DDL can be optimized not only by the drug concentration but also by TEOS amount used during the preparation of Ptx-SiO2 NPs process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Incorporation of CDs NPs into the silica shell around the Ptx-SiO2 microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve the fluorescence properties, a biocompatible and photo-stable material (CDs) have been used in this work. The synthesized CDs are highly soluble in ethanol and also possess amid group (Fig. 1) which polymerize with TEOS to form the silica shell.

The Fig. 5. (a and b) of SEM images for the synthesized Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 shows that the NPs are spherical in shape with a smooth surface, average particle size of 100 nm, and relatively narrow size distribution. For further evidence, the Ptx-SiO2 @CDs-SiO2 NPs were also observed with TEM. The TEM images (Fig. 5. (c, d)) show an apparent silica shell (marked by arrows in Fig. 5. (c)), which confirm the successful silica shell growth around the silica core Ptx-SiO2, with shell thickness about 10 nm without influencing the spherical shape of the silica core (Ptx-SiO2). The synthesized Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs possess a smaller particle size compared with the prepared NPs from the literature [46], this due to the used Ptx-SiO2 instead of the bare silica NPs in the reported work, and controlling the shell thickness by optimizing the TEOS amount during the shell growth step. It has been previously proved that smaller particle size is superior with regard to tumor accumulation, penetration, and cellular internalization [49].
In order to achieve the best fluorescence properties both of CDs and TEOS amount were optimized during the silica shell growth process.

As shown Fig. 6 the yellowish solution on the left corresponding to the prepared CDs in the bright field, possess a high blue photoluminescence properties when observed under UV light at 365 nm, and also the synthesized Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs which form white suspension in the bright field and it has a blue fluorescence under the UV light in the same condition with CDs solution (365 nm), and the Ptx-SiO2 has a negative result under UV light at 365 nm, which confirms that the CDs have been successfully incorporated into the silica shell around the prepared SiO2-Ptx NPs with conserving the fluorescent properties of the used CDs, since the silica materials are optically transparent and inert [47].

For further evidence, the fluorescence microscopy image (Fig. 6. (c)) observed by the confocal laser optical microscope with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm for the dried and re-dispersed Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs in ethanol, confirm that the microspheres are with suitable bright for the bioimaging application.

The in vitro drug release was carried out in three sets of experiments in simulation to in vivo situation (PBS pH 7.4, pH 5.0 and fetal bovine serum/PBS pH 7.4 at 37 °C with gentle stirring). The first one was to compare the drug release from Ptx-SiO2 and Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2, the second set consist to study Ptx release at different pH (PBS pH 7.4 normal physiological conditions, pH 5.0 intracellular conditions of cancer cells) from Ptx-SiO2 NPs. Furthermore, the drug release was studied in plasma medium. In all sets, biphasic release behavior was observed for both nanoparticles (Ptx-SiO2 and Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2). Generally, the cumulative drug release results show no burst release and highly controllable release, since the release kinetics is determined by diffusion from the center of the Ptx-SiO2 NPs. Which might reduce the undesirable dose dumping, side effects and also leads to long-term therapeutic efficacy. [6,48]
As shown in Fig. 7. (A), the drug release was faster in Ptx-SiO2 than Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs, which is in agreement with both molecules structures, the release was more sustained
in Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 due to the presence of the silica shell, after 6 days the release amount in both carriers will be closed due to the decomposition of the silica networks in the shell and
the core, in addition to the negligible thickness of the silica shell compared with silica core, therefore, the presence of the silica shell did not affect the drug release from these NPs.

In the second set, the drug release from Ptx-SiO2 at pH 7.4 and pH 5.0 was described in Fig. 7. (B), in which the drug release was faster at pH 5 than pH 7.4 from the beginning to the end of the release. Like it can be observed in that figure the drug release at pH 5.0 attend 95% after 9 days when, the drug release at pH 7.4, attend only 75% which confirm the pH-sensitive of Ptx release from Ptx-SiO2 NPs, and this may be due to the facile silica networks decomposition and consequently Ptx drug molecules easily escape at acidic pH.

In the third set, the drug release from Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 in different medium PBS with pH 7.4 and serum (fetal bovine serum/ PBS pH 7.4, 50/50 v) have been studied. As shown Fig. 7. (C) the drug release rate was lower than in PBS due to the protein coating absorbed on the surface of Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 when using the serum as medium [16]. After 5 days of drug release in serum, Ptx concentration was less than 40%, which means that the concentration of Ptx in blood will be low compared with intra-cancer cells (PBS pH 5.0), which significantly increase the dosage of Ptx in the intra-cell region and might reduce side effects of Ptx during the chemotherapy.

In order to further understanding of Ptx release from these NPs (Ptx-SiO2 and Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2), the release kinetics and mechanism were analyzed by data fitting to the above models (zero-order model; Eq. (3), first order model ; Eq. (4), Higuchi model; Eq. (5) and Korsmeyer–Peppas model; Eq. (6)). The correlation coefficients, R2 and the rate constants calculated from the release kinetics analysis, are summarized in Table 2. From the different analysis data represented in Table 2, it can be seen that the in vitro release from different materials (Ptx-SiO2 and Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2) follows the Higuchi model. This is due to the higher correlation coefficient obtained from this model’s equation. This indicates that the Ptx release from the silica carriers is a diffusion controlled process. [50] To verify the release mechanism, the in vitro release results were fitted to the Korsmeyer Peppas Eq. (6). The values of n and k were calculated for each release profile, and summarized in Table 2. The log value of the percentage of drug release was plotted as a function of the log time according to the equation. If n = 0.5, the drug diffuses and releases from the silica carriers according to Fickian diffusion. For n > 0.5, irregular or non-Fickian type drug diffusion occurs. If n = 1, completely non-Fickian or case II release kinetics is running. [51] The n value is around 0.226 for Ptx release from Ptx-SiO2 NPs at pH 7.4. Which indicate that the Ptx release mechanism from these NPs is a Fickian diffusion, which is controlled by an interaction. Contrary to the Ptx release from Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 n was calculated to be found 0.665 which is non-Fickian diffusion.

The morphological evolution of Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 (sample D3 with a degree of drug loading of 7.09%) was observed by using TEM ( Fig. 8. (a-d)). Here no significant change was noted on the shape after 1 day of immersion of Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 in PBS (pH 7.4), then after 4 days of immersion (Fig. 8. (b)) apparent pores (marked by arrows) have been observed in the center of the NPs, these pores continuously enlarge in the following days leaving thinner layers of silica. After 15 days of immersion, most of the nanoparticles are damaged leaving a weak structure, which can be easily decomposed into small fragments for easier excretion from the bloodstream after drug release. Finally, the drug release from the center of the NPs into the outside medium determined by diffusion was the main cause of carrier decomposition. This unique aspect leads to prolonged drug residence in the bloodstream and avoid the carrier renal filtration before the drug release [16,41].

Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was used to investigate the cellular uptake of Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 followed by imaging of CDs. As shown in Fig. 9, after incubation of A549 cells with the Ptx-SiO2 NPs for 2 h, only the blue fluorescence of cell nuclei stained by DAPI [40] was observed under excitation wavelengths of 400 nm, 440 nm and 500 nm indicating that the Ptx-SiO2 NPs did not have the ability to reflect laser signals with a specific laser beam. On the contrary, when A549 cells were incubated with fluorescent Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2, the cells emitted blue following excitation at 400 and 450 nm, red fluorescence at 500 nm was observed inside the cell. However, the red fluorescence was reflected by Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 in the merge was distinct, which indicated that the Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 nanocarriers could be taken up by A549 cells due to the hydrophilic surface of SiO2 NPs which is favorable for cellular uptake [18], and also the small size of Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs (around 100 nm). Furthermore, became localized in the nucleus to kill cancer cells.
The biocompatibility of the prepared SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs was evaluated, by incubating it with A549 cells at different concentrations for 24 and 48 h ( Fig. 10).

Cytotoxicity is one of the most important issues for the biomedical application of nanomaterials. Previous reports have demonstrated that both of SiO2 and CDs are nanoparticles which induce little cytotoxicity [40,52,53].

From Fig. 10, it can be seen that the cell viability values of SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs show a small decrease when the concentration is less than 20 μg/mL (the cell viability was more than 80%). Even when the concentration reached 100 μg/mL after 24 or 48 h of incubation the cell viability was greater than 70%. These preliminary results demonstrate that SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs possess desirable biocompatibility and low toxicity. Therefore, this makes them good candidates for both in vitro and in vivo biomedical applications.

Biodegradable silica core and highly fluorescent and eco-friendless carbon dots have been used to fabricate core-shell silica nanospheres. Paclitaxel was successfully loaded into the silica core with drug molecules highly concentrated in the NPs center. The silica networks were entangled with the drug molecules in the nanoparticles. The results show that the drug escape leads to the silica NP decomposition into small fragment for safe renal excretion. The prepared silica core-shell possesses a high photo-luminescent property, a spherical shape with small particle size (~100 nm), good water solubility. The slow release of Ptx from these NPs leads to a longer half-life of Ptx for enhanced overall efficacy. The cellular uptake performance of Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 labeled with the Ptx-SiO2 NPs has been evaluated by confocal microscopy. The results showed that Ptx-SiO2@CDs-SiO2 NPs exhibit a high cellular uptake and could emit blue and red fluorescence at different excitation wavelengths. These properties in addition to the biocompatibility and biodegradability of the fabricated silica core-shell nanospheres make these materials as promising nanocarriers for anticancer drug delivery and cell bio-imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are delighted to acknowledge discussions with colleagues in their research group.

ce:section.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
